channel.title: Balance on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Balance on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Temporary Buffs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about temporary buffs.

And I mean. What I mean by that are the buffs you get for a short period of time for having a spell cast on you or drinking a potion or maybe eating food in an RPG. This is based on a large set of questions asked by future Astrominer. I’m going to read all their questions, then I’m going to talk about it a little bit and then I’m going to go through each question in order and answer it. This is going to be long and it’s going to reference a lot of videos, but that’s actually something I need to talk about after I read you the questions. So let’s start.

item.title: Min Player Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about min player spec.

You may wonder what I’m talking about. This is a complicated topic. I’ve put off talking about it literally for years because it is complex and it is nuanced And I’ve thrown out those words before in previous video topics that I thought were had had that nuance to it. People still took the wrong thing, so I’m going to be very clear and careful. Also, this is a topic I don’t talk about with a lot of other game developers because it requires a certain amount of experience actually making games before you get why this is important. I don’t hear game players talk about this much at all.

item.title: Action Points, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about action points, part two.

I did a video a few weeks ago on action points, Generated a lot of really good discussion in the comment section, including this comment from Matamwit who asked: I love this topic. I would like to ask what everyone thinks about, and then there’s five topics. What One?

What advantages and disadvantages are there if we separate, split movement points and action points in two separate pools? Number two: what do you think about barring action points from the next turn? Three: what about allowing one character to sacrifice their own points for another party member to take action with? Four: what do you think about skills and spells that require more action points than you currently have available and the character has to spend multiple turns if they want to perform the action? This is a good opportunity to lock more powerful skills behind multiple turn blocks and force opponents to focus the character that is preparing a bigger scout — uh, bigger spell. And number five: higher action point pools which allow for perks and items that increase the pool by a small amount without impacting game balance.

item.title: Unneeded Realistic Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unneeded realistic designs.

What I mean by this is I was talking to a friend who’s actually in the game industry — never worked with him. So industry distant colleague — and he asked me a very simple question. He said: “How come you’ve never really had weather in a lot of your games”. And I started saying things like: well, it wasn’t connected, I didn’t connect it to anything And we did other things for visual variation, but really full screen effects can be expensive, whether 2D or 3D, for different reasons, which I’ll talk about. And he said: well, you did time of day in a lot of your games. There was time of day in Fallout, there was time of day in Arcanum. And Arcanum one mattered.

item.title: Game Simplification
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game simplification, and by this I’m talking about various ways games can be made easier to use but to some players feel handh holdy.

This is me answering a question from VA cus who asks: recently I saw some screenshots from games with very obvious Clues where to go next. I mean like obvious gray rock formations with obvious yellow protrusions to stick to ancient ladders painted in yellow, Etc. I don’t actually know from what games those were.

item.title: Balancing Friction
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about balancing friction, and this comes from a question from padon Roso — I hope I’m pronouncing your name correctly — who asked: can you talk about balancing friction in RPGs and games?

It seems like quite the complex thing to do, when the friction points for the player and how they’re distributed around the gameplay Loop will most likely influence other already complex systems in the game. For example, I’m thinking about pacing, both the storytelling and the alternation of action, non-action faces. I’m also talking about game economy, as talked about in the encumbrance video, and the full effect of friction points seems to me like something you can only fully evaluate when the game is all playable, especially when the crossover with those other systems I was referring to earlier is taken into account. Oh wait, there’s more. Do you have any trick or story you can tell about this, about how you tackle this problem along your career?

item.title: Balancing Game Features
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about balancing game features.

Now this is a question from Josh and Parker who asked: could you go over the process of balancing new game features as they’re added to the game? How do you make sure new weapons or spells aren’t overpowered but the player is still able to get enough new content to stay entertained? How do you know how many hits an enemy should take to kill Etc?

item.title: Gameplay Options
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gameplay options now.

A few months ago I did a video on game difficulty settings and talked about the pros and cons of different difficulty settings you should put in the game. But in the comment section a bigger discussion happened about just gameplay options in general — way more than just difficulty settings — and one of the things that came up was people talking about games that have a lot of gameplay settings. Gameplay options now there are people who like them and they like them for the obvious reasons of you know. I like to be able to customize the game exactly how, I want to play it, which I get, and I probably fall in that category a lot more than the people who do not like having lots of gameplay options.

item.title: Damage Numbers, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about damaged numbers again, because I already did a video on damage numbers, but that video was mostly about how they should appear in the HUD.

And apparently what a lot of you wanted was: how do you actually pick damage numbers? And by that you meant the range of damage numbers, because once you have a range of damage numbers — like do you go from 1 to 10, one to 20, one to 100, one to a th000? Once you pick that range, that leads to a r, a subsequent range of damage, of numbers for hit points and bonuses and weapon damage and things like that. So I’ve talked about damage numbers as a HUD. I’ve also talked about the ranges you should use for skills and attributes. This talk is going to be very similar because a lot of the things I said in the skill and attribute talks, which I’ll link below, are still valid here. Um, so, before I go about talking about the method, I use to do numbers, number ranges for damage, let me talk about just some stuff about numbers. Big numbers make people happy, when they hit something and they see two, they’re not as happy as when they see 20 or 200 or 2,000 or 20,000. Now you may go wait, if the monster has 10 hit points, who cares if you’re doing two versus the monster has 10,000 hit points and you do 2,000?

item.title: What Is Fun?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fun.

I’ve already done a video, and I’ll link it below, about balancing fun and realism in games, but I’ve got a lot of fun oriented questions. People want to know what I think about fun versus balance, what I think about games that force you to go, what the designer considers to be the F path, or just what is fun, Define fun. I’m going to take a stab at it same way I took a stab at def: finding what an RPG is. Let’s see how this goes, first of all, let’s talk about fun versus balance. I don’t think anybody’s going to be surprised that I prefer fun overbalance in any of my games, and you can even see some of my games were really unbalanced.

item.title: Game Difficulty Settings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game difficulty.

Specifically, I got a question from Noah Calder 4279, who asks: I’d love if you could share your thoughts on difficulty in RPGs. Do you think customizable difficulties such as settings and Sliders work well in RPGs? Which are the best methods of making RPGs difficult, and should RPGs get easier or more difficult the further a player advances through the game? That is a set of good questions that all fall under game difficulty. So I thought, okay, time to do a game difficulty video. So just up front, I do like customizable difficulty settings. I think anything that makes a player turn the game into something they’d like more I’m not against. Frequently in my career I’ve had arguments with people — designers, programmers — who think that every time you put an option in a game it’s because you couldn’t make a decision, and I see where they’re coming from. There were sometimes I couldn’t decide what was better and I thought, maybe.

item.title: Level Scaling
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about level scaling.

Had a lot of questions about level scaling, especially after I did some of the videos on Random Encounters and how I did it in both Fallout and Arcanum, so I thought I’d talk about level scaling and how my ideas on it changed over time. But let me go ahead and uh give one of the questions that many, many people asked. This one is from Angel vodka 2984, who asks: what do you think about level scaling? Do you like the game world and enemies to scale up to your characters level so you get more or less constant challenge, or do you prefer a fixed level system in which some areas get easier over time but you get a more real sense of progression?
